title_zh: 亚历克斯·卡普走向战场

paragraphs.en: Alex Karp and I would not seem to have much in common. I work for WIRED, which does tough reporting on Trumpworld; Karp is the CEO of Palantir, a $450 billion firm that has contracts with agencies like the CIA and ICE and worked for the Israeli military during its campaign in Gaza. I live in the East Village of New York City, and the home Karp spends the most time in is a 500-acre compound in rural New Hampshire. (Last year he was one of the highest paid executives in the United States.) I was a plain old English major, and he’s got a law degree and a PhD in philosophy, studying under the legendary Jürgen Habermas. I consider myself a progressive; Karp regards that stuff as “pagan religion.”
paragraphs.zh: 我和亚历克斯·卡普（Alex Karp）看起来似乎没有太多共同点。我在《连线》（WIRED）工作，专门做对特朗普圈层（Trumpworld）的硬核报道；卡普是Palantir的首席执行官，这是一家市值高达4500亿美元、与美国中央情报局（CIA）和国土安全移民及海关执法局（ICE）等机构有合同，并在以色列对加沙行动中为其军方提供过支持的公司。我住在纽约市的东村，而卡普最常待的住所是一处位于新罕布什尔州乡村的500英亩产业园。去年他是美国报酬最高的高管之一。我是个普通的英语专业出身，而他拥有法学学位和哲学博士学位，曾师从享有盛名的尤尔根·哈贝马斯（Jürgen Habermas）。我自认是进步派，而在他看来，那一套就是“异教信仰”。

paragraphs.en: But we can bond over one shared status: Both of us are alumni of Central High School, a Philadelphia magnet school. (Not at the same time. I have some years on the 58-year-old executive.) Maybe it was that connection that led Karp to agree to a sit-down. The son of a Jewish pediatrician and a Black artist, Karp struggled with dyslexia, and at Central he seems to have turned a corner—even speculating now that overcoming the challenge helped position him for later success.
paragraphs.zh: 但我们有一个共同点：我们都毕业于费城的一所文理中学——中央高中（Central High School）。（当然不是同一年；我比这位58岁的高管年纪更轻一些。）也许就是这段关系让卡普同意了这次会面。卡普出生于一位犹太小儿科医生和一位黑人艺术家的家庭，在童年和青少年时期与读写障碍（dyslexia）搏斗。来到中央高中后，情况似乎有所转折，他现在甚至推测，正是战胜这种挑战，让他后来更容易走向成功。

paragraphs.en: We conducted our interview at an annual gathering of Palantir’s corporate customers. The event had the giddy vibe of a multilevel marketing summit. The customers I talked to—from giants like American Airlines to relatively modest family firms—said that Palantir’s AI-powered systems are expensive but well worth it.
paragraphs.zh: 我们在Palantir年度企业客户聚会中进行采访。那场活动有一种像多层次营销峰会（multilevel marketing summit）那样的兴奋氛围。与会者中的客户——从美国航空（American Airlines）这样的巨头到规模较小的家族企业——都说Palantir的人工智能驱动系统虽然昂贵，但物有所值。

paragraphs.en: Not presenting at the event are the customers who provide Palantir with the majority of its business—the US government and its allies. (The company does not do business with Russia or China.) Palantir was founded to put Silicon Valley’s innovation into defense and government technology. With coauthor Nicholas Zamiska (a Palantirian), Karp laid out his philosophy earlier this year in a book called The Technological Republic, a surprisingly readable polemic that skewers Silicon Valley for insufficient patriotism. In Karp’s view, the antiestablishment tone of Apple’s Macintosh marketing was the original sin in a tech culture that celebrates indulgent individualism and neglects nationalist concerns. At the conference, Karp, dressed in a white T-shirt and jeans, began his opening remarks by saying, “We’ve been at odds with Silicon Valley on and off since our inception 20 years ago.” In 2020, Karp moved the company headquarters from Palo Alto to Denver, whereupon it became that state’s wealthiest corporation.
paragraphs.zh: 未在活动上亮相的是Palantir主要业务来源的客户：美国政府及其盟友。（该公司不会与俄罗斯或中国开展业务。）Palantir的成立初衷就是把硅谷的创新能力带入国防和政府科技。卡普与联合作者尼古拉斯·扎米斯卡（Nicholas Zamiska）在今年早些时候出版了《技术共和国》（The Technological Republic），这本出人意料地可读的论辩性著作猛烈抨击硅谷缺乏爱国意识。在卡普看来，苹果“Macintosh”营销中那种反建制的基调是技术文化的原罪，它把放纵的个人主义神话化，却忽视了民族国家层面的关切。在会议上，卡普穿着白色T恤和牛仔裤，开场时便说：“我们成立20年来，与硅谷时而对立、时而交锋。” 2020年，卡普把公司总部从帕洛阿尔托（Palo Alto）迁到丹佛（Denver），自那时起，Palantir就成为该州最富有的公司。

paragraphs.en: Some see Karp as a dystopic supervillain. He responds to those critics aggressively, bluntly, and without a shred of remorse. After years of contracts, the company has apparently proven to the government’s satisfaction that its tools can effectively leverage information on the battlefield and in intelligence operations. Palantir has a multimillion-dollar contract with ICE involving “targeting and enforcement”—essentially helping the agency to locate people for deportation. In Ukraine, Karp says with pride, the company’s products have helped deliver lethal force. Palantir has a Code of Conduct that supposedly binds the company to, among other things, “protect privacy and civil liberties,” “protect the vulnerable,” “respect human dignity,” and “preserve and promote democracy.” In an open letter last May, 13 former workers accused Palantir’s leadership of having abandoned its founding values and of being complicit in “normalizing authoritarianism under the guise of a ‘revolution’ led by oligarchs.” Karp has also revealed that other employees have left because of the company’s work with the Israel military. His retort: If you’re not generating opposition, you’re probably doing something wrong.
paragraphs.zh: 有人把卡普看作反乌托邦式的超级反派。他对这些批评者的回应一向强硬、直接，而且毫无悔意。经过多年合同合作，该公司似乎已经向政府证明了其工具在战场和情报行动中有效利用信息的能力。Palantir 与美国国土安全部移民及海关执法局（ICE）签有一份数百万美元的合同，内容是“瞄准与执法”——本质上是帮助该机构定位待遣返人员。在乌克兰，卡普自豪地说，公司产品帮助实施了致命打击。Palantir 还制定了《行为准则》，据称要求公司做到诸如“保护隐私与公民自由”“保护弱势群体”“尊重人的尊严”以及“维护并促进民主”。在去年五月的一封公开信中，13 名前员工指控 Palantir 领导层抛弃了创始价值观，并且在寡头主导的“革命”名义下，与“威权主义的正常化”合谋。卡普还透露，其他员工也因公司与以色列军方的合作而离职。他的反驳是：如果你没有引发反对声，那你大概在做错了什么。

paragraphs.en: Beneath his fiery defense of Palantir, I sense that Karp yearns to be understood. He noted that all anyone wants to talk to him about is ICE, Israel, and Ukraine. I wanted to visit those subjects, too, and we did. But our conversation also touched on Donald Trump, democracy, and his love affair with German culture. Oh, and Central High.
paragraphs.zh: 在卡普为 Palantir 辩护的激烈言辞下，我感到他其实渴望被理解。他提到，人们都想和他谈论的只有 ICE、以色列和乌克兰。我也想谈这些，而且我们也谈到了。但我们的对话同样涉及唐纳德·特朗普、民主，以及他对德国文化的迷恋。还有中学时的中央高中。

paragraphs.en: STEVEN LEVY: I understand that your experience at Central High in Philadelphia was transformational.
paragraphs.zh: STEVEN LEVY：我了解到，你在费城的中央高中经历具有改变人生的意义。

paragraphs.en: ALEX KARP: They gave me an IQ test for the mentally gifted program. My parents had never bothered to tell me to get all A’s. But when the person in charge, Mrs. Snyder, got my IQ test she told me, “Clearly you’re dyslexic, but someone with your IQ can’t get B’s, you have to get all A’s.” That’s when I went from a strong student to an exceptional one. She kind of changed my life.
paragraphs.zh: ALEX KARP：他们给我参加了天赋计划的智商测试。我的父母从来没有催促我拿到全 A。我都记得那位主管斯诺登夫人拿到我的智商测试结果后说：“显然你是个阅读障碍者（dyslexic），但以你的智商，不可能只拿 B，你必须拿全 A。” 就是从那时起，我从一名成绩优秀的学生，变成了一个杰出的学生。她算是改写了我的人生。

paragraphs.en: A lot of people say that it isn’t clear exactly what Palantir does. Can you explain it in your own words?
paragraphs.zh: 很多人都说帕拉提尔到底做什么并不清楚。你能用自己的话解释一下吗？

paragraphs.en: If you’re an intelligence agency, you’re using us to find terrorists and organized criminals while maintaining the security and data protection of your country. Then you have the special forces. How do you know where your troops are? How do you get in and out of the battlefield as safely as possible, avoiding mines, avoiding enemies? Then there’s Palantir on the commercial side. The shorthand is if you’re doing anything that involves operational intelligence, whether it’s analytics or AI, you’re going to have to find something like our products.
paragraphs.zh: 如果你是一个情报机构，你会用我们的服务来查找恐怖分子和有组织犯罪分子，同时维护国家的安全和数据保护。然后就是特种部队。你怎样知道你部队的位置？你如何尽可能安全地进出战场，避开地雷、避开敌人？此外，在商业领域也有帕拉提尔。简而言之，如果你正在做任何涉及作战情报的事情，不管是分析还是人工智能，你都需要类似我们产品这样的工具。

paragraphs.en: Basically, it’s about orchestrating information with AI, which is something lots of companies in Silicon Valley want to do. But you contend that no other tech company can do it like yours.
paragraphs.zh: 本质上说，就是用人工智能来编排信息，这正是硅谷许多公司想做的事。但你们坚持认为没有其他科技公司能像你们那样做到。

paragraphs.en: What I’m really saying is we know how to do it. If you find someone else who can do it, and you don’t want to work with us, buy it from them.
paragraphs.zh: 我真正的意思是，我们知道自己会做。如果你找到别的公司能做到，而且你不想和我们合作，那就向他们购买。

paragraphs.en: Is there anyone you consider a competitor?
paragraphs.zh: 你有没有觉得谁是我们的竞争对手？

paragraphs.en: Our competition is political. The woke left and the woke right wake up every day, figuring out how they can hurt Palantir. If they get into power, they’ll hurt Palantir. And by the way, it’s global. I view Democrats as my party, but if the Mamdani wing of the party takes over? If that’s my party, I’m not in it. Or the right woke wing, which is like, everything is a conspiracy, any use of technology is actually going to only be used to eviscerate and attack us? Palantir is literally the hardest software to abuse in the world, but they don’t seem to want it. If you don’t want meritocracy, you hate Palantir. That is our competition.
paragraphs.zh: 我们的竞争对手是政治。觉醒左翼和觉醒右翼每天都在想方设法伤害 Palantir。若他们掌权，他们就会伤害 Palantir。顺便一提，这可是全球性的。我把民主党视作我的阵营，但如果民主党里出现了 Mamdani 派掌权？那如果真是我的阵营，我就不在其中。或者右派的觉醒派，认为一切都在阴谋中，任何技术使用最终只会被拿来掏空并攻击我们？Palantir 在世界上可以说是最难被滥用的软件，但他们似乎就是不想要它。如果你不认同精英制，就会讨厌 Palantir。这就是我们的竞争。

paragraphs.en: My head’s spinning a little. How did Mamdani get in there?
paragraphs.zh: 我的脑子有点晕。Mamdani 究竟是怎么进来的？

paragraphs.en: If you explain to the world that labor is going to be valueless, people are going to elect the most ridiculous people ever. They’re afraid of an AI-driven, AGI environment where no one has a job. Universities and elite institutions have played a corrosive role here. People are teaching pagan religion views—a new religion with sacrifices. Who’s the sacrifice? Me, I’m the sacrifice.
paragraphs.zh: 如果你向世界解释说劳动会变得没有价值，人们就会选出有史以来最荒诞的人物。他们害怕的是一个由人工智能推动、通用人工智能（AGI）时代的世界，在那里没有人有工作。大学和精英机构在这方面起了腐蚀作用。人们在传播一种异教式的观念——一种以献祭为核心的新宗教。那个被献祭的人是谁？是我，我就是那个被献祭的人。

paragraphs.en: You don’t seem constrained to me. You’re doing great.
paragraphs.zh: 你在我看来并没有被束缚，进展得很好。

paragraphs.en: I’m not complaining. We’re not victims at Palantir. We’re doing very well. We might even do better.
paragraphs.zh: 我不是在抱怨。我们在 Palantir 不是受害者。我们的表现非常好，甚至可能更好。

paragraphs.en: In your book you offer a harsh critique that tech companies are not sufficiently patriotic.
paragraphs.zh: 在你的书里，你对科技公司不够爱国提出了严厉批评。

paragraphs.en: In the beginning, we were at odds with Silicon Valley because we were pro-American, pro-West, and pro-making the government functional. That was very controversial in Silicon Valley because it equated to not making any money and being a loser. We won that battle. I think Silicon Valley actually has become, at least behind closed doors, patriotic. Silicon Valley has always been pro-meritocracy, and we’re very aligned with that. Where we’re currently at a misalignment-alignment apex is that we believe in using large language models in a way that creates actual empirical value, but also is very strong for workers. We walked toward the government when everyone walked away. That’s how we ended up powering Maven, the US government’s AI battlefield plan.
paragraphs.zh: 起初，我们与硅谷对立，因为我们是亲美、亲西方的，并且主张让政府正常运转。这在硅谷当时非常有争议，因为那等于放弃盈利、被视为失败者。我们赢得了那场争论。我认为硅谷其实已经在某种程度上转变了，至少在闭门场合里变得更有爱国意识。硅谷一向推崇精英主义/绩效导向，我们与此高度一致。我们当前最大的分歧在于：我们相信应当以能带来真实、可验证价值的方式使用大语言模型，而且这种方式对劳动者也非常有利。众人离开政府时，我们却走向了政府。正是这样，我们最终参与了支持 Maven——美国政府的 AI 战场方案。

paragraphs.en: You have been part of the war in Ukraine from the start. What have you learned from that about the battlefield of the future?
paragraphs.zh: 你从乌克兰战争伊始就参与其中。关于未来的战场，你从中学到了什么？

paragraphs.en: I can tell you off the record.
paragraphs.zh: 我可以先给你一个不留名的回答。

paragraphs.en: This is a Q and A, so that wouldn’t be helpful.
paragraphs.zh: 这是一次问答，所以那样说反而不够有用。

paragraphs.en: Then I’ll give you the on-the-record version, which is almost the same. Broadly speaking, at the beginning of the war it was very clear that you would need software orchestration of small objects. But then the Russians began jamming all devices. The lesson was that the only thing that basically mattered was how to get through the jam space and get your device to deliver its payload. So it’s the ability to interact with the device, plan where the device is going, and gather intel from the device. That’s very different from how war has been fought in the past.
paragraphs.zh: 那我给你一个可以公开说的版本，内容几乎一样。总的来说，在战争开始时，大家都很清楚：你会需要对小型装备进行软件编排。后来俄罗斯人开始对所有设备进行干扰。由此得到的教训是，基本上最重要的只有一件事——如何穿透干扰环境，让你的设备把载荷成功传递出去。也就是说，关键是能够与设备互动、规划设备的去向，以及从设备中获取情报。这个与过去的战争方式非常不同。

paragraphs.en: Is this a scarier form of war?
paragraphs.zh: 这是一种更可怕的战争形态吗？

paragraphs.en: The way I look at it is: Is America going to be scarier or are our adversaries going to be scarier? You need high-end satellites, you need to be able to coordinate with the satellites, and you need software and large language models. It’s advantageous for America because we’re very strong in those areas.
paragraphs.zh: 我的看法是：是美国会更可怕，还是我们的对手会更可怕？你需要高端卫星，你需要能够与卫星协同工作，还需要软件和大型语言模型。美国在这些领域非常强大，这让我们处于优势。

paragraphs.en: Palantir has a unique culture—some even call it a cult. I wonder how much you cultivate the outsider mentality. We’re both from Philadelphia; Jason Kelce after the first Super Bowl win was saying, “No one likes us. We don’t care.”
paragraphs.zh: Palantir 有一种独特文化——甚至有人称之为“教派”。我在想，你到底有意识地培养了多少“局外人”心态。我们都来自费城；杰森·凯尔斯在赢得第一场超级碗之后曾说过："没人喜欢我们，我们不在乎。"

paragraphs.en: People don’t understand that there’s a massive feature side to being an outsider. It’s not pleasant to be unpopular. I don’t like it, actually. But you get the best people in the world. If someone says something ridiculously stupid about Palantir, five people look at it and the fifth person says, hey, it can’t be as simple as that—this is a really interesting company, and they’re thinking of the 10th derivative of a problem, not the simplistic bullshit from someone online who has no idea how our products work. That’s exactly the kind of person you want inside your business, or buying your product someday, or investing in you. There’s no country in the world where our brand is as bad as in France, but we have the best French employees in the world, working at a company that’s apparently a CIA front, which is obviously complete bullshit.
paragraphs.zh: 人们不明白，“局外人”身份有一个非常重大的优势面。变得不受欢迎并不舒服。我其实并不喜欢这样。但也正因为如此，你才能聚集到世界上最优秀的人才。有人对 Palantir 说出荒唐到离谱的话时，第五个人就会出来说：等等，这不可能这么简单——这是一家非常有意思的公司，他们在思考问题的第十阶导数，而不是那种在线上胡乱评论却完全不懂我们产品怎么运作的人的肤浅废话。这正是你在公司里希望拥有的人，也正是未来可能购买你产品、或投资你的人。世界上没有哪个国家对我们的品牌评价像在法国那样差，但我们却有世界上最出色的法国员工，他们在一个被误认为是“CIA前台公司”的公司里工作，而那显然完全是胡说八道。

paragraphs.en: You may not be a CIA front, but you work for the CIA and ICE.
paragraphs.zh: 你可能不是中情局（CIA）的幌子组织，但你确实为CIA和移民及海关执法局（ICE）工作。

paragraphs.en: ICE came later. Our first contracts were with US intelligence, our biggest contracts ended up being in the DOD, and then later we started working in Homeland Security under Democrats. Now it’s very controversial because it’s Trump.
paragraphs.zh: ICE是后来才加入的。我们最早的合同来自美国情报部门，后来最大的合同都落在国防部（DOD）那边，随后我们在民主党执政时期开始为国土安全部（Homeland Security）提供服务。如今之所以引发巨大争议，是因为现在是特朗普。

paragraphs.en: It’s controversial because Trump is doing things that we haven’t seen done before, particularly with ICE. You have a corporate code that supports democracy and stands against discrimination. Do you track whether your products are being used in ways that violate that code? Is there a line beyond which you would say no to a president?
paragraphs.zh: 之所以有争议，是因为特朗普正在做前所未有的事，尤其是在ICE问题上。你们有一套支持民主、反对歧视的企业准则。你们是否会追踪自己的产品是否被用于违反这套准则的用途？有没有一条红线是你会对总统说“不”的？

paragraphs.en: I was the first CEO to say we would not build a Muslim database. [Editor’s note: Technically he was not the first, but considering Palantir’s business, the statement was consequential.] I’ve defended Israel, so I think I’m the last person in the world people would expect to say that. I can’t go into details. But I’ve pulled things from places in the United States where I thought there was something going on like that.
paragraphs.zh: 我曾是第一个宣布我们不会建立“穆斯林数据库”的首席执行官。[编辑注：从技术上说他并非第一个，但就Palantir的业务性质而言，这一表态具有重要意义。] 我公开支持以色列，所以我想大概是世界上最不可能被人预期会说这句话的人。我不能透露细节，但我确实从美国的某些地方撤回了一些项目，因为我认为那里发生了类似的事情。

paragraphs.en: Maybe you and I disagree about ICE because I spent most of my adult life in Europe, in Germany. I am an immigration skeptic. I personally think that citizens have to decide by their vote what immigration policy is going to be. I grew up in a progressive family, but people my age and older were massive immigration skeptics. Open borders is not a progressive policy; it never was. Look at what a version of open borders has done to Germany on any vector.
paragraphs.zh: 也许你我对 ICE 看法不同，因为我成年后大部分时间都在欧洲，尤其是德国度过。我对移民问题持怀疑态度。我个人认为，移民政策应当由公民通过投票来决定。我在一个进步家庭长大，但我这一代和更年长的一代人却是移民问题的高度怀疑者。开放边界并不是一个进步立场；它从来都不是。你看看“开放边界”在德国各个方面都造成了什么。

paragraphs.en: Let me ask again: Are you monitoring what’s happening with the US government and democracy in this country, and asking yourself if you need to take a closer look at how your products are being used?
paragraphs.zh: 我再问一遍：你有在关注美国政府与民主制度在这个国家发生了什么，并问自己是否需要更仔细地审视你们的产品是如何被使用的吗？

paragraphs.en: Let me reframe that. Have I ever worked against our commercial interests because it violated our norms? Yes. Have I done this in government? Yes. We get no credit for this, but we almost went out of business because we were not working in Russia or China. Do I agree with your implicit assertion that what’s going on in immigration as you formulate it has never been done before? No. Do you know what happens when you’re in Japan, and you overstay your visa for one minute? You are put on a plane. [Ed. note: The Japanese government has received domestic and international criticism for its policy of indefinitely detaining thousands of immigrants.] I do think this is a very valuable line of questioning you’re asking—if our product allowed for civil rights abuses, would I intervene? Yes, though our product is the hardest in the world to violate. Do I agree with some of the assertions you just made? No. But you’re bringing up exactly the right question, and I’m telling you that I have done this, and I will continue to do it.
paragraphs.zh: 我换个方式重述一下。我的确因为违背公司原则而反对过与商业利益相冲突的做法吗？是。我的确在政府里做过这样的事吗？也是。我们因此很少得到认可，但我们几乎快要倒闭，因为我们当时没有在俄罗斯或中国开展业务。你隐含地断言，按你现在讲的移民问题从未有过前例吗？不是这样。你知道在日本，如果签证超期一分钟会怎样吗？你会被直接送上飞机。[编者注：日本政府因对数千名移民实行无限期拘留的政策，遭到国内外批评。] 我认为你提出的这个问题非常有价值——如果我们的产品会被用于侵犯公民权利，我会不会介入？会，但我们的产品是全世界最难被滥用的产品之一。你刚才提到的一些说法我并不认同。但是你提的正是关键问题，我要告诉你的是，我已经这样做过，而且还会继续这么做。

paragraphs.en: Does your Jewish background inform the support Palantir is giving to Israel?
paragraphs.zh: 你的犹太背景是否影响了 Palantir 对以色列的支持？

paragraphs.en: I don’t give to Israel. I allow them to purchase our product. Look, you’re asking whether we are defined by where we sit or by our ideas? I always think it’s both. I view myself as an outsider. There’s obviously a particularistic element, no doubt, but I would like to believe that a support of Western values and their robust defense includes something that’s not just me doing a derivative of my own identity.
paragraphs.zh: 我没有给以色列捐款。我只允许他们购买我们的产品。你看，你是在问我们是由立场决定，还是由理念决定？我一直认为两者都有。说实话我觉得自己是个外来者。毫无疑问，里面当然有一种特殊利益取向，但我想相信，对西方价值观的支持以及对这些价值观的坚定捍卫，不只是我在复制自己某一种身份。

paragraphs.en: A lot of Jews, me included, feel affinity to Israel but also feel that what’s happening in Gaza is intolerable.
paragraphs.zh: 许多犹太人，包括我在内，都对以色列有认同感，但也会认为，加沙正在发生的事情是无法忍受的。

paragraphs.en: If you have five Jews, you have 50 opinions. The issue for Palantir is, do you support Israel? Not do you support every micro decision that Israel is making?
paragraphs.zh: 如果你身边有五个犹太人，就会有五十种观点。Palantir 面临的问题是：你是否支持以色列？而不是你是否支持以色列正在做的每一个具体小决策。

paragraphs.en: We’re not talking about a little thing here.
paragraphs.zh: 我们讨论的不是一件小事。

paragraphs.en: Israel is a country with a GDP smaller than Switzerland, and it’s under massive attack. Some critiques are legitimate, but others are aggressive in attacking Israel. My reaction is, well, then I’m just going to defend them. When people are fair to Israel and treat it like any other nation, which I don’t think they do, I will be much more willing to express in public the things I express in private to Israelis.
paragraphs.zh: 以色列的国内生产总值还不如瑞士大，但却遭受着大规模攻击。有些批评是合理的，但也有一些是在对以色列进行进攻性的抨击。我的反应是，嗯，那我就只能站出来为它们辩护。当人们以公正的态度对待以色列，并像对待任何其他国家一样看待它时——我认为他们目前并没有这么做——我就会更愿意把我私下对以色列人表达的观点，在公开场合也说出来。

paragraphs.en: When you get a letter from 13 former employees saying that Palantir’s leadership has failed to stand up to authoritarianism, do you take that seriously?
paragraphs.zh: 当你收到13名前员工的公开信，他们说Palantir的领导层没有挺身反对威权主义时，你会认真对待这种说法吗？

paragraphs.en: You have to steelman everything that’s lobbed at you. When The New York Times says we’re eviscerating human rights, blah, blah, blah, that’s complete malarkey. Everyone knows it’s malarkey. They know it’s malarkey. I certainly steelman [the critiques from] ex-Palantirians and current ones. Anybody making decisions all day is wrong sometimes. But if you attack me irresponsibly, that will harden my view.
paragraphs.zh: 你必须对任何向你投来的指责先给出“最强版本”的理解。纽约时报如果说我们正在摧毁人权等等一类话，那完全是胡说八道。大家都知道那是胡说八道，他们也知道那是胡说八道。我当然会认真对待前员工和现任员工的批评。一个整天都在做决策的人有时会犯错。但如果你不负责任地攻击我，这只会让我的立场变得更坚定。

paragraphs.en: Do you have a relationship with Trump?
paragraphs.zh: 你和特朗普有关系吗？

paragraphs.en: It depends how you define “relationship.” He’s the president of the United States, and I very much respect that. I agree with him on some things, and probably on some things I don’t.
paragraphs.zh: 这要看你如何定义“关系”。他是美国总统，我非常尊重这一点。我在有些问题上同意他的看法，也有一些问题上可能和他意见不同。

paragraphs.en: I think he’s done a much better job than you think he’s done.
paragraphs.zh: 我认为他做得比你认为的要好得多。

paragraphs.en: In the positioning of your question, you’re not neutral. Talking to you is just like talking to my family. It’s like where me and my family disagree. And I’ll tell you where those places are. They’re on the border, they’re in Israel, and they’re in Ukraine. I get yelled at all day about those three issues.
paragraphs.zh: 在你这个问题的表述中，你并不中立。和你谈话就像和我的家人谈话，就像我和我的家人在某些问题上存在分歧一样。我会告诉你这些分歧在哪些地方。它们在边境、在以色列，以及在乌克兰。我每天都在围绕这三个问题被人责骂。

paragraphs.en: I’d like to meet your parents!
paragraphs.zh: 我很想见见你的父母！

paragraphs.en: I think if we were family members, in private you and I would disagree, and I would be pointing out that Trump’s decisions on AI, and his decisions on the Middle East, are very different than people in the Democratic Party would have made, and very good.
paragraphs.zh: 我想如果我们是亲属关系，在私下里你我肯定会持不同看法，而且我会指出，特朗普在人工智能问题上的决定，以及他在中东问题上的决定，与民主党人会做出的选择非常不同，而且非常正确。

paragraphs.en: Let’s go back in time a bit. You are an unusual CEO. You aren’t technical. You went to law school at Stanford and didn’t practice but went to Germany for a PhD in philosophy. What do you think Peter Thiel saw in you that led him to choose you to run his defense-related startup?
paragraphs.zh: 我们先回到过去一点。你是个不寻常的首席执行官。你不是技术背景出身。你在斯坦福上了法学院，但没有执业，而是去德国读了哲学博士。你认为彼得·蒂尔在当初为什么会选择你来经营他与国防相关的创业公司？

paragraphs.en: God only knows, but I would say my best explanation is the same things that made Peter the world’s best value investor—he finds people that understand the sixth, seventh, eighth derivative of a problem in a business context. And we were friends. I do think there’s a Germanic overlap, in our aptitude for understanding the consequences of a decision very far out. There’s a lot of Germanic culture in Palantir—the whole process of going deeper and deeper and deeper into a problem, what we call the Five Whys, is very Germanic. The level of rigor to understand if something is plausibly true, or at least figure out if it’s definitively wrong, is definitely a derivative of my education. I would say our high standard of quality is somewhat derivative of authors I read and people I dealt with in Germany.
paragraphs.zh: 天知道为什么，但我想我最好的解释和他成为世界最佳价值投资人的原因一样——他能找到那些懂得在商业语境下把问题剥开到第六、第七、第八层“导数”的人。我们曾是朋友。我确实觉得我们在能力上有一种日耳曼式的重叠，即能理解决策后果在很远未来的样子。Palantir 的整个流程都带着这种日耳曼气质：一层一层更深地追问问题，这就是我们所谓的“五个为什么”。要想确认一件事是否有可信度，或者至少弄清它是否明确错误，那种严谨程度，确实是我教育背景的一种衍生。可以说，我们那种很高的质量标准，也在很大程度上源自我在德国读过的作家和遇到的人。

paragraphs.en: It seems that Germany resonates with you personally.
paragraphs.zh: 似乎德国与你个人有很深的共鸣。

paragraphs.en: I just did very well there. In Germany, let’s just say you meet a girl—you don’t have to run around saying you love them after, like, the first date. You may go out with someone for years, and you could just be honest, like you really care about them. There’s very little white-lie-telling in Germany.
paragraphs.zh: 我在那边做得非常好。比如在德国，咱们就这么说：你可能会遇见一个女孩儿，第一次约会后并不需要到处奔走去说“我爱你”。你可以和一个人交往多年，始终只是坦诚地表达你真的在乎他/她。德国几乎没有那么多“善意的谎言”。

paragraphs.en: I suspect you don’t tell too many white lies in the United States.
paragraphs.zh: 我猜在美国，你也不会说太多“善意的谎言”。

paragraphs.en: There’s a higher price for not doing it. I do bring some of that Germanic culture to America, but I do think America is a superior culture.
paragraphs.zh: 只是不这么做，你也要付出更高的代价。我确实把一些日耳曼式的文化带到了美国，但我确实认为美国是一个更优秀的文化。

paragraphs.en: You seem to live your life the way you want. Not many CEOs live in some remote corner of New Hampshire like you do.
paragraphs.zh: 你似乎是在按自己想要的方式生活。没多少首席执行官会像你这样住在新罕布什尔州某个偏僻的角落。

paragraphs.en: Doing what everybody else wants me to do is much harder for a dyslexic like me than a non-dyslexic, so I don’t think I’d be very good at it. Maybe I’m not everyone’s cup of tea, but I kind of like being me on most days.
paragraphs.zh: 做别人都想让我做的事，对我这样一个阅读障碍的人来说，比对普通人更难，所以我认为我不会做得特别好。也许我不是人人都喜欢的类型，但我在大多数时候都挺喜欢做自己这个人的。

paragraphs.en: You are a fanatical cross-country skier. What do you think about when you do that for hours at a time?
paragraphs.zh: 你是一个狂热的越野滑雪爱好者。你一口气滑上几个小时时，会在脑子里想些什么？

paragraphs.en: Have your sayLet us know what you think about this article in the comments below. Alternatively, you can submit a letter to the editor at [email protected].
paragraphs.zh: 请发表您的看法。请在下方评论区告诉我们您对本文的意见。或者，您也可以给编辑部写信：[email protected]。
